title: Gaussian Blur for Denoising
concept: Gaussian blur is a weighted average where nearby pixels count more than distant ones, giving a smoother, more natural result than a flat box average — a good match for smoothing out Gaussian-style noise.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is median filtering so effective against salt-and-pepper noise?

options.text: It picks the middle value of the neighborhood, so a single extreme outlier pixel gets outvoted
It deletes every pixel that isn't pure black or white
It only looks at the single pixel itself, ignoring all neighbors

exercises.explain: Right — the median ignores outliers. But median filtering isn't the go-to tool for every kind of noise. Today: a filter suited to noise that isn't a handful of specks, but a shimmer across every pixel.

cards.title: Why photo apps call it "Gaussian Blur" specifically
cards.body: Open an editing app's blur menu and you'll often see "Gaussian Blur" listed by name, not just "Blur." That's not marketing fluff — it's a specific, deliberate way of averaging pixels together, chosen because it tends to look smoother and more natural than a plain box average.

cards.title: What is Gaussian blur?
cards.body: A basic blur (like in an earlier lesson) averages every neighbor equally. Gaussian blur instead uses a weighted average: the center pixel and its closest neighbors count the most, and the weight fades smoothly the further away a neighbor sits — following a bell-curve pattern.

cards.title: A spotlight instead of a floodlight
cards.body: A plain average is like a floodlight shining evenly across the whole neighborhood — every pixel gets equal say. A Gaussian blur is more like a soft spotlight: pixels near the center get the brightest, strongest influence, while ones near the edge of the window barely register at all.

cards.title: Smoothing away a Gaussian shimmer
cards.body: A photo with Gaussian noise has every pixel nudged up or down by a small random amount. Blending each pixel with a weighted mix of its close neighbors averages those random nudges toward zero, so the shimmer fades — while the smooth center-weighting keeps the result looking more natural than a boxy flat average.

cards.title: It's still a tradeoff, not magic
cards.body: The bell-curve weighting makes Gaussian blur look smoother than a flat box average, but it's not a free upgrade. It's still averaging pixels together — push it too hard and it washes out real detail and edges just like any other blur, Gaussian or not.

cards.title: Box blur weights vs. Gaussian blur weights
cards.body: How much each neighbor counts toward the new pixel value:

left.text:
Every neighbor weighted equally
Flat weighting across the window

right.text:
Center weighted most, fading outward
Bell-curve weighting

exercises.prompt: How does Gaussian blur differ from a basic box blur?

options.text: It weights closer neighbors more heavily than distant ones, following a bell-curve pattern
It only looks at a single pixel with no neighbors at all
It picks the median value instead of averaging
It ignores the center pixel entirely

exercises.explain: Correct — Gaussian blur is a weighted average where nearby pixels count for more than distant ones.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In a Gaussian blur, every neighboring pixel contributes exactly the same amount to the result, just like a box blur.
exercises.explain: False. That's how a box blur works. Gaussian blur weights closer neighbors more heavily, fading out toward the edge of the window.

exercises.sentence: Gaussian blur weights pixels near the center the most, with influence fading in a ___ pattern.
exercises.explain: Bell-curve is correct — that smooth, center-heavy fade-off is exactly what Gaussian weighting means.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes Gaussian blur as a denoising tool?

options.text: A weighted average that smooths out random per-pixel shimmer while looking more natural than a flat box average
A filter that only deletes scattered black-and-white pixels, leaving the rest untouched
A filter that sharpens edges by exaggerating pixel differences

exercises.explain: That's exactly it — a smooth, center-weighted average built to calm down random shimmer like Gaussian noise.

exercises.prompt: A photo has soft, all-over Gaussian noise, not scattered black-and-white specks. Why might Gaussian blur be a better first choice here than median filtering?

options.text: Gaussian blur's weighted averaging directly cancels out small random shifts spread across every pixel, which is exactly what Gaussian noise is
Gaussian blur only works on salt-and-pepper noise, never on any other type
Median filtering can only be applied to black-and-white images
There's no real difference — both filters always produce identical results

exercises.explain: Exactly — different noise, different tool: weighted averaging suits an all-over shimmer, while the median suits scattered outliers. Next: what happens if you push denoising too far?
